title: Is This AI Fair to You?

modules.title: What Ethics Actually Means for AI
modules.sub: Grounding ethical thinking in real AI contexts
modules.description: Learners explore what the word 'ethics' genuinely means when applied to automated systems — beyond buzzwords and headlines. They distinguish between ethical principles and legal rules, and begin building a personal framework for evaluating AI behavior in everyday life.

modules.title: Who Decides What AI Values?
modules.sub: Tracing the human choices embedded in AI systems
modules.description: Learners investigate how the values baked into AI systems reflect the priorities of the people and organizations who build and fund them. They examine real examples of design decisions — from training data selection to optimization targets — and consider whose voices are typically included or excluded in that process.

modules.title: Fairness: Easy Word, Hard Problem
modules.sub: Unpacking why fairness means different things to different people
modules.description: Learners work through competing definitions of algorithmic fairness and discover that satisfying one definition can mathematically conflict with another. They apply these definitions to practical scenarios — such as hiring tools and loan approvals — to feel the real tension between fairness goals.

modules.title: Transparency and the Right to Know
modules.sub: Demanding explainability from systems that affect your life
modules.description: Learners examine what it means for an AI system to be transparent and why opacity is often the default in commercial and government contexts. They assess what meaningful explanation looks like in practice and identify what questions citizens and consumers can reasonably demand answers to.

modules.title: Accountability When AI Causes Harm
modules.sub: Identifying who is responsible when automated decisions go wrong
modules.description: Learners map the chain of responsibility across developers, deployers, regulators, and end users when an AI system produces harmful outcomes. They analyze real cases where accountability was diffused or denied, and practice identifying the leverage points where meaningful responsibility can and should be assigned.

modules.title: Privacy as a Human Right
modules.sub: Connecting data collection to dignity and personal autonomy
modules.description: Learners reframe privacy not as a technical compliance issue but as a foundational human right that AI systems routinely put under pressure. They evaluate how data collection, behavioral profiling, and surveillance technologies erode personal autonomy, and consider what protections they are entitled to expect.

modules.title: Ethics in Action: Real Case Studies
modules.sub: Applying your ethical toolkit to documented real-world AI failures
modules.description: Learners work through a set of documented AI ethics cases — spanning healthcare, criminal justice, hiring, and content moderation — using the frameworks and vocabulary built throughout the course. They practice diagnosing what went wrong, which principles were violated, and what a more ethical alternative might have looked like.

modules.title: Becoming an Ethical AI Citizen
modules.sub: Turning critical awareness into everyday advocacy and action
modules.description: Learners consolidate their knowledge into a personal action plan for engaging with AI ethics beyond the course — as consumers, employees, voters, and community members. They identify concrete ways to ask better questions, push back on opaque systems, and contribute to a more accountable AI environment in their own professional and civic lives.

synopsis: AI systems make consequential decisions about loans, jobs, healthcare, and news every single day — but who is ensuring those decisions are fair? This beginner-friendly course gives you the practical vocabulary and critical thinking tools to question, evaluate, and advocate around AI ethics in your daily life.
rationale: A demand signal of 7/10 paired with a corpus similarity of only 0.60 against a single advanced 'Ethics Framework' course reveals that accessible, everyday-language ethics education is missing for the general public who most needs it.
